scores.reasoning: The strongest engineering foundation. The sim-unit-band + one-frame similarity rescale (S=0.2) is the only proposal that GUARANTEES full float32 precision at every scale forever, with a mathematically pixel-identical rescale frame. SoA typed arrays, flat-array spatial hash, write-once ball-local stuck instances (zero per-frame cost for 500 stuck objects), deterministic chunk spawning with consumed bitmasks, and concrete measurable budgets (draw calls, tris, ms, GC bytes) make it the most implementable spec. Weaknesses: gameplay feel is under-specified (no attach animation, knock-off off-by-default, no audio, no camera spring), and its coupling of rescale to tier-up means the population transition logic must be carefully kept off the rescale frame.

Contains the single most important DESIGN insight: seamlessness is structural when absorbability, camera, fog, and speed are continuous functions of radius and tierIndex drives only cosmetics (content bands, palettes, celebration). Its feel inventory — attach animation squash, spring camera with look-ahead, FOV kicks, knock-off bounce, WebAudio sfx, tuning.js as the designer's file, dynamic resolution governor — is what makes the game a showcase rather than a tech demo. Weaknesses: the 3-frame crust-bake pipeline is its riskiest and least necessary component (multi-ms mergeGeometries spikes, scheduler constraints, drift mitigations — all solving a problem the ring-buffer + burial-cull approach dissolves), and Map-based hashes/object records are weaker than SoA for the hot path.

Valuable as a razor: tiers gate CONTENT, never coordinates; one instance state machine (FREE/WORld/SPAWNING/DESPAWNING/STUCK/EJECT) unifying all object lifetimes; burial culling as 'the bake in 5 lines'; zero-scale matrices instead of compaction; dev keys (?r=5, forced rebase) for testability; hysteresis on tier boundaries. But its core precision bet — no rescale, meters forever, near/far scaling with r, floating-origin only — is the weakest of the three at the top of the scale range (r=150m puts fog-range content ~50km from a 2km-rebase origin in float32) and caps growth headroom; variable-dt physics invites tunneling it then has to patch; per-frame stuck matrix recompute does work the parented-pool approach gets for free; and 30 archetypes / 5 tiers is the thinnest content spec.

decisionsAndRationale: BASE = Proposal 1 (PERFORMANCE-FIRST). WHY: it is the only proposal whose float-precision mechanism (sim-unit band [0.5,2.5] + one-frame similarity rescale S=0.2) guarantees full Float32 precision at EVERY scale with unbounded growth headroom, and its SoA/flat-array/zero-GC discipline is what makes 4000+ objects at 60fps on integrated GPUs credible rather than hopeful. Proposal 3's no-rescale scheme degrades at the top of the range (fog-range content tens of km from origin in float32) and caps growth; Proposal 2's rescale agrees with P1's, confirming the approach.

GRAFTED Proposal 2's governing law: tierIndex drives ONLY cosmetics and spawn-content selection; absorbability (objRadius <= 0.65*ballRadius), camera, fog, speed, and despawn are continuous functions of radius. WHY: this makes seamlessness STRUCTURAL — there is no gameplay event at a threshold that could pop or hitch — which is a stronger guarantee than tuning transitions to be unnoticeable. P1 already mostly complied (its 0.62 absorb rule was continuous); we made it explicit and absolute.

Kept rescale COUPLED to tier-up (P1) rather than independent (P2). WHY: it makes per-tier sim-unit configs (hash cell sizes, load radii, chunk sizes) compile-time constants, and the mandatory hash rebuild lands on the one frame where it is unavoidable anyway. The decoupling P2 wanted is preserved where it matters: gameplay never branches on the tier event, and population changes are amortized around (never on) the rescale frame.

REJECTED Proposal 2's crust-baking pipeline; adopted P1's write-once ball-parented stuck pools + ring buffer 512 + Proposal 3's burial cull (attachR + objHalf < 0.98*currentR, staggered over ~1s). WHY: crust baking is 3-4ms main-thread mergeGeometries with scheduler constraints ('never same frame as rescale') and socket-drift mitigations — all solving a problem that burial culling dissolves in ~5 lines at zero per-frame cost; the noise-displaced ball core preserves the 'history' feel. KEPT P2's 0.15s attach animation — it is the money shot and costs one tween per absorb.

Custom arcade physics, no library — the one decision all three proposals made independently. WHY: the only dynamic body is a kinematic sphere on a plane vs static spheres; rapier (~1.7MB wasm, async init, breaks under teleport-scaling every body at rescale) and cannon-es (slow JS solver, fights arcade tuning) solve absent problems; custom is ~400 LOC, deterministic, and makes the knock-off mechanic trivial. Fixed 60Hz accumulator (P1/P2) over P3's variable dt, which invites the tunneling bug P3 then patches.

Three per-live-tier SpatialHash instances (P3's structure) with P1's flat-typed-array counting-sort implementation. WHY: 3 live tiers span ~25x object size; one shared hash forces query reach = ballR + maxSceneryR, exploding the cell range. Per-tier cells keep queries at 9-25 cells. P1's flat arrays beat P2/P3's Map<string,int[]> on allocation and cache behavior.

Zero-scale matrices + high-water count for dead instances (P3) instead of P1's swap-compaction. WHY: simpler, removes instanceSlot bookkeeping churn on the moved object, and degenerate triangles are rejected pre-raster; combined with r177 updateRanges partial uploads (P2's mitigation) the bandwidth concern is handled.

Knock-off on hard bonk (eject 1-3 newest stuck objects, re-absorbable) promoted from P1's 'stretch goal, off by default' to core (P2/P3 both included it). WHY: it is a signature Katamari feel beat, it is nearly free under custom physics, and it gives collisions a consequence that makes obstacles read as gameplay rather than geometry.

Feel layer is first-class: tuning.js as the single designer file, spring camera with look-ahead, FOV kicks, dynamic-resolution governor, WebAudio-synthesized sfx, effects.js — all from P2. WHY: P2's top-listed risk is correct — scale-invariant proportional design can make growth feel flat; the camera-spring lag, odometer unit rollovers, palette shifts, and looming next-tier scenery are the counters, and they must be tunable without touching system code. Camera spring state is stored in sim space and rescaled by S at tier-up to preserve the pixel-identity guarantee.

Catalog: 48 archetypes (8 x 6 tiers, P1's count over P2's 36 / P3's 30), with P2's per-archetype collisionScale fudge for long/flat objects. WHY: content variety is the visible richness of the showcase and the workpackage is perfectly parallel (one developer, pure functions, zero dependencies); collisionScale is the cheap fix for sphere-proxy oddities on benches/buses, deferring multi-sphere proxies unless playtests demand them.

Testability grafts from P3 baked into the spec: ?r= start-at-any-radius dev key (instant tier testing), force-rescale key with screenshot diff (proves the identity transform), forced-rebase teleport key (floating origin can't rot), dev-mode asserts for the fog < loadRadius invariant and spawn determinism across rebase. WHY: the two riskiest mechanisms (rescale frame, origin rebase) fire rarely in normal play; without forced-trigger keys they would be untested until a player hits them.

File layout: 28 small modules, P1's tree + P2's tuning.js/effects.js/sfx.js/mathUtils.js and public/_headers; all interfaces frozen in src/types.js during a half-day Phase 0 so 5 developers work in parallel against typedefs, with ObjectStore and InstancedPool flagged as the two lead-controlled contracts because most modules touch them.
